tiles.name: Shinsekai
tiles.funFact: Built in 1912 as a "new world" modeled after New York and Paris, Shinsekai is famous for its retro atmosphere and kushikatsu deep-fried skewers.

tiles.name: Tennoji
tiles.funFact: Tennoji Park was established in 1909 and houses the Osaka Municipal Museum of Fine Art with over 8,000 works of Japanese and Chinese art.

tiles.name: Midosuji Line
tiles.funFact: Opened in 1933, the Midosuji Line is Osaka's busiest subway line carrying over 1.2 million passengers daily along the iconic Midosuji Boulevard.

tiles.name: Namba
tiles.funFact: Namba is Osaka's vibrant entertainment hub, home to the famous Namba Grand Kagetsu theater where yoshimoto comedians have performed since 1912.

tiles.name: Shinsaibashi
tiles.funFact: The Shinsaibashi-suji shopping arcade stretches 600 meters and has been Osaka's premier shopping street since the Edo period in the 17th century.

tiles.name: Amerikamura
tiles.funFact: Known as "Amemura," this youth culture district emerged in the 1970s when shops began importing American vintage clothing and records.

tiles.name: Tsutenkaku
tiles.funFact: The current 108-meter Tsutenkaku Tower was rebuilt in 1956 after the original 1912 tower was dismantled for steel during World War II.

tiles.name: Kansai Electric Power
tiles.funFact: Founded in 1951, Kansai Electric Power supplies electricity to over 13 million customers across the entire Kansai region of western Japan.

tiles.name: Sumiyoshi Taisha
tiles.funFact: Founded in 211 AD, Sumiyoshi Taisha is one of Japan's oldest shrines and the headquarters of over 2,300 Sumiyoshi shrines nationwide.

tiles.name: Osaka Castle
tiles.funFact: Originally built by Toyotomi Hideyoshi in 1583, the castle's main tower stands 55 meters tall and played a key role in Japan's unification.

tiles.name: Osaka Loop Line
tiles.funFact: The Osaka Loop Line completed its circular route in 1961, connecting 19 stations over 21.7 kilometers around central Osaka.

tiles.name: Dotonbori
tiles.funFact: Named after merchant Yasui Doton who funded the canal in 1612, Dotonbori's famous Glico Running Man sign has been an icon since 1935.

tiles.name: Kuromon Market
tiles.funFact: Known as "Osaka's Kitchen" for over 190 years, Kuromon Market has around 150 shops specializing in fresh seafood, produce, and street food.

tiles.name: Umeda
tiles.funFact: The Umeda Sky Building's Floating Garden Observatory at 173 meters was named one of the world's top 20 architectural structures by a travel guide.

tiles.name: Universal Studios
tiles.funFact: Universal Studios Japan opened in 2001 and attracts over 14 million visitors annually, making it the most visited amusement park in Japan.

tiles.name: Tempozan
tiles.funFact: At just 4.53 meters, Mount Tempozan is one of Japan's lowest officially recognized mountains, created from soil dredged from Osaka harbor in 1831.

tiles.name: Kaiyukan
tiles.funFact: Osaka's Kaiyukan aquarium holds 10,941 tons of water and is home to whale sharks, manta rays, and over 30,000 marine creatures from the Pacific Rim.

tiles.name: Nankai Line
tiles.funFact: The Nankai Main Line, established in 1885, is one of Japan's oldest railway lines and connects Osaka to Kansai International Airport and Wakayama.

tiles.name: Nakanoshima
tiles.funFact: This sandbar island between the Dojima and Tosabori rivers has been Osaka's cultural center since 1891, housing the National Museum of Art.

tiles.name: Kitashinchi
tiles.funFact: Kitashinchi is Osaka's most exclusive dining and entertainment district with over 3,000 bars, clubs, and restaurants in a compact area.

tiles.name: Osaka Water Bureau
tiles.funFact: Osaka's water system draws from Lake Biwa through a 20 km canal built in 1895, providing clean water to over 2.7 million residents daily.

tiles.name: Abeno Harukas
tiles.funFact: At 300 meters, Abeno Harukas was Japan's tallest skyscraper from its 2014 completion until 2023, featuring a museum, hotel, and observation deck.

tiles.name: Expo Park
tiles.funFact: Site of Expo '70, the first World's Fair in Asia with 64 million visitors! The iconic Tower of the Sun by Taro Okamoto still stands at 70 meters.

tiles.name: Minoh Falls
tiles.funFact: Minoh Waterfall cascades 33 meters and is one of Japan's top 100 waterfalls, famous for its spectacular autumn foliage and maple leaf tempura.

tiles.name: Sakai
tiles.funFact: Sakai is home to the Daisen Kofun, the largest keyhole-shaped burial mound in the world at 486 meters long, built in the 5th century for Emperor Nintoku.

tiles.name: Keihan Line
tiles.funFact: The Keihan Line has connected Osaka and Kyoto since 1910, running premium double-decker cars on its 49.3-kilometer main line.

tiles.name: Grand Front
tiles.funFact: Grand Front Osaka opened in 2013 as part of the massive Umeda North Yard redevelopment with 266 shops, a knowledge center, and intercontinental hotel.

tiles.name: Kita
tiles.funFact: The Kita district is Osaka's northern commercial heart, anchored by Osaka Station City which serves over 2.5 million passengers daily.
